title: श्री दुर्गा द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री दुर्गा द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम् ॥

ॐ दुर्गा दुर्गार्तिशमनी दुर्गापद्विनिवारिणी।
दुर्गमच्छेदिनी दुर्गसाधिनी दुर्गनाशिनी॥ १॥

दुर्गतोद्धारिणी दुर्गनिहन्त्री दुर्गमापहा।
दुर्गमज्ञानदा दुर्गदैत्यलोकदवानला॥ २॥

दुर्गमा दुर्गमालोका द्वादशैतानि नामतः।
यः पठेत् शृणुयाद्वापि दुर्गादेव्या मनोरथान्॥ ३॥

सर्वान् पूरयते नित्यं विशेषात् सुखदायिनी।
न तस्य मृत्युभयं वा न शत्रुभयं क्वचित्॥ ४॥

नमो दुर्गे जगन्मातः सर्वशक्तिस्वरूपिणी।
रक्ष रक्ष जगन्मातरीशानि त्राहि नः सदा॥ ५॥

नमस्ते शैलपुत्री च नमस्ते ब्रह्मचारिणी।
चन्द्रघण्टायै नमस्तुभ्यं कूष्माण्डायै नमो नमः॥ ६॥

स्कन्दमातर्नमस्तुभ्यं कात्यायनि नमोऽस्तु ते।
कालरात्र्यै नमस्तुभ्यं महागौर्यै नमो नमः॥ ७॥

सिद्धिदात्र्यै नमस्तुभ्यं दुर्गादेव्यै नमो नमः।
द्वादशैतानि नामानि सर्वसिद्धिप्रदानि च॥ ८॥

॥ इति श्री दुर्गा द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Durga Dvadash Nama Stotram ||

Om Durga Durgartishamani Durgapadvinvarini.
Durgamachchhedini Durgasadhini Durganashini. || 1 ||

Durgatoddharini Durganihandri Durgamapaha.
Durgamajnanada Durgadaityalokadavanala. || 2 ||

Durgama Durgamaloka Dvadasaitani Namatah.
Yah Pathet Shrinuyadwapi Durgadevya Manorathan. || 3 ||

Sarvan Purayate Nityam Visheshat Sukhadayini.
Na Tasya Mrityubhayam Va Na Shatrubhayam Kvachit. || 4 ||

Namo Durge Jaganmatah Sarvashaktiswarupini.
Raksha Raksha Jaganmatarishani Trahi Nah Sada. || 5 ||

Namaste Shailaputri Cha Namaste Brahmacharini.
Chandraghantayai Namastubhyam Kushmandayai Namo Namah. || 6 ||

Skandamatarnamastubhyam Katyayani Namostu Te.
Kalaratryai Namastubhyam Mahagauryai Namo Namah. || 7 ||

Siddhidatryai Namastubhyam Durgadevyai Namo Namah.
Dvadasaitani Namani Sarvasiddhipradani Cha. || 8 ||

|| Iti Shri Durga Dvadash Nama Stotram Sampoornam ||
hindiMeaning: दुर्गा द्वादश नाम स्तोत्र में माँ दुर्गा के बारह दिव्य नाम हैं: दुर्गा, दुर्गार्तिशमनी, दुर्गापद्विनिवारिणी, दुर्गमच्छेदिनी, दुर्गसाधिनी, दुर्गनाशिनी, दुर्गतोद्धारिणी, दुर्गनिहन्त्री, दुर्गमापहा, दुर्गमज्ञानदा, दुर्गदैत्यलोकदवानला, दुर्गमा और दुर्गमालोका। इन नामों को पढ़ने या सुनने से सभी मनोरथ पूर्ण होते हैं। मृत्यु और शत्रुओं का भय नहीं रहता। नवदुर्गा - शैलपुत्री, ब्रह्मचारिणी, चन्द्रघण्टा, कूष्माण्डा, स्कन्दमाता, कात्यायनी, कालरात्रि, महागौरी और सिद्धिदात्री को नमन करने से सर्वसिद्धि प्राप्त होती है।
benefits: Reciting 12 names of Durga fulfills all wishes
Traditionally recited for removal of fear of death and enemies completely
Includes Navadurga names for complete divine protection
Invokes all siddhis and spiritual attainments
Traditionally recited for removal of poverty, difficulties, and demonic forces
description: The Durga Dwadash Nama Stotram, known in Sanskrit as श्री दुर्गा द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम्, is a devotional hymn of praise in praise of Goddess Durga. This sacred composition is sourced from Devi Bhagavata Purana and continues to inspire devotees worldwide. Regular recitation is believed to include Navadurga names for complete divine protection, remove fear of death and enemies completely, and grant all siddhis and spiritual attainments. This sacred text is ideally recited on Tuesday (Mangalvar) and Friday (Shukravar) and during Navratri and Durga Puja for enhanced blessings. With 8 verses and a reading time of about 5 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Durga Dwadash Nama Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Durga Dwadash Nama Stotram - 12 sacred names of Maa Durga in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Powerful prayer for protection and fulfillment.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Durga Dwadash Nama Stotram and what are its twelve names?
seoFaqs.answer: The Durga Dwadash Nama Stotram (श्री दुर्गा द्वादश नाम स्तोत्रम् — dwadash = twelve, nama = name) is a short Sanskrit hymn enumerating the twelve sacred names of Goddess Durga, traditionally preserved in the Devi Bhagavata Purana and the Durga Saptashati appendix tradition. The twelve names commonly enumerated are: (1) Durga, (2) Durgarti-shamani (remover of difficult sufferings), (3) Durgapadvinivarini (one who removes the calamity of difficulties), (4) Durgamachhedini (cutter of impassable obstacles), (5) Durgasadhini (accomplisher of the impassable), (6) Durganashini (destroyer of difficulties), (7) Durgatoddharini (uplifter from difficult states), (8) Durganihantri (slayer of obstacles), (9) Durgamapaha (remover of the impassable), (10) Durgamajnanada (giver of knowledge of the difficult), (11) Durgadaityalokadavanala (forest-fire to the world of demons), (12) Durgama (the impassable herself). (Sources: Devi Bhagavata Purana; Durga Saptashati tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the famous closing verse Sri Durge Smritiha Sarvopadrava Nashini?
seoFaqs.answer: The closing verse of the Durga Dwadash Nama Stotram declares the phalashruti: Naitani namani dvadasha — These twelve names; bhakta-pathena yah pathet — whoever recites with devotion; Durge smritiha sarvopadrava-nashini — the very remembrance of Durga is the destroyer of all calamities. The verse promises that simple devoted recitation of these twelve names brings about the destruction of every kind of upadrava (calamity, distress, sudden misfortune) — physical, mental, financial, relational and spiritual. This verse is often inscribed on Durga yantras and recited as a daily protective mantra by women, travellers and those facing crisis. The complete phalashruti continues with promises of protection during travel, in forests, in difficult terrains and in moments of acute danger. (Sources: Durga Dwadash Nama Stotram phalashruti; Devi Bhagavata.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the difference between Durga Dwadash Nama and Navadurga?
seoFaqs.answer: These are two distinct enumerations of Durga's forms, though they overlap. The Durga Dwadash Nama is a list of twelve sacred names of Durga — all of them variations on the theme of difficulty (durga) being removed by the goddess (durga = the impassable; she who removes the impassable is therefore Durga). The Navadurga is a different enumeration of nine specific manifestations of Durga: Shailaputri, Brahmacharini, Chandraghanta, Kushmanda, Skandamata, Katyayani, Kalaratri, Mahagauri and Siddhidatri — each worshipped on one of the nine nights of Navaratri. The Dwadash Nama is more about Durga's rescuing function (removing 12 kinds of difficulty); the Navadurga is more about her cosmic manifestations (9 forms across her life-cycle as Devi). Both are recited during Navaratri. (Sources: Devi Mahatmya; Durga Saptashati; Navadurga tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When is the Durga Dwadash Nama Stotram traditionally recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The Durga Dwadash Nama is recited daily by Devi devotees during the morning Devi sandhya, during all nine nights of both Sharad Navaratri (Ashwin Shukla, September-October) and Chaitra Navaratri (Chaitra Shukla, March-April), every Tuesday (Mangalvar — Devi's day) and Friday (Shukravar — also sacred to Devi), every Ashtami and Navami of every month (Devi-tithis), Durga Ashtami specifically, before commencing any difficult journey or hazardous undertaking (since the stotra is renowned as a travel-protection prayer), before any major examination, court appearance or confrontation, during difficult life-phases, before sleeping in unfamiliar places, by women returning home late at night, and as a daily protective mantra by traditional Hindu women across India. (Sources: Drikpanchang Devi calendar; Devi Bhagavata; classical Devi paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: Why are all twelve names variations of the word Durga?
seoFaqs.answer: All twelve names are variations on the word Durga because the very name Durga embodies the goddess's central function: the Sanskrit root durga means difficult, impassable, hard-to-cross, fortress-like — referring originally to a fortified place difficult to enter, then by extension to any difficult situation, calamity, distress or suffering. Devi is called Durga because she is both the impassable fortress (the protective shelter the devotee can flee to) and the slayer of the impassable (the one who removes every difficulty). The twelve names play with this etymology: Durgarti-shamani (peace-giver in difficulty), Durgapadvinivarini (remover of difficulty-fall), Durgamachhedini (cutter of the impassable), Durgasadhini (accomplisher of the difficult), and so on. The repetition reinforces the goddess's identity as the cosmic remover of every impassable obstacle. (Sources: Sanskrit etymology; Devi Bhagavata; Durga Saptashati.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Durga Dwadash Nama Stotram grant?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition attributes the following benefits to regular Durga Dwadash Nama Stotram recitation: protection during travel through forests, mountains, dangerous terrains and unfamiliar places (the stotra is the classical travel-protection prayer of Hindu tradition), removal of all twelve categories of upadrava (calamity) — physical injury, financial loss, emotional grief, family discord, enemy harm, wild animal attack, fire, water, weapon, theft, disease and untimely death, success in any difficult undertaking that seems impassable (durgama-sadhana), removal of obstacles in business, career, marriage and family, blessings of courage and fearlessness, protection from negative spiritual forces, blessings for women's safety especially in vulnerable situations, fulfilment of cherished wishes that seemed impossible, and the supreme phala — the goddess's grace and ultimately moksha through her power. (Sources: Durga Dwadash Nama phalashruti; Devi Bhagavata; classical Devi tradition.)
